channel.title: Expression on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Expression on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Bash Conditional Expression Error
item.description:
Bash Conditional Expression Error Fix

Conditional expression errors occur when [[ ]] has invalid syntax, uses wrong operators, or has unexpected word splitting on unquoted variables.

What This Error Means

[[ ]] is the bash-specific extended test command. It supports regex matching, pattern matching, and logical operators but still requires correct syntax. Errors include malformed expressions and operator misuse.

Common Causes

  • Using = instead of == for regex/pattern matching
  • Unquoted regex pattern containing spaces
  • Mixing -a/-o with &&/||
  • Using (( )) arithmetic inside [[ ]] incorrectly
  • Missing operands for operators

How to Fix

1. Use == for pattern/regex matching

# WRONG: = is for string equality
if [[ $var == ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then

# RIGHT: use == (same, but == is conventional for patterns)
if [[ $var =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then

2. Quote regex patterns

# WRONG: unquoted pattern
if [[ $var =~ ^[a-z]+$ ]]; then

# RIGHT: quote to prevent special character interpretation
if [[ "$var" =~ ^[a-z]+$ ]]; then

3. Use && and || instead of -a and -o

# WRONG: deprecated
if [[ $a -gt 1 -o $b -lt 10 ]]; then

# RIGHT: use logical operators
if [[ $a -gt 1 || $b -lt 10 ]]; then

4. Handle empty variables

# [[ ]] doesn't word-split, but be safe
if [[ -n "${var:-}" ]]; then
 echo "var is set and non-empty"
fi

Related Errors

  • Conditional Expression — conditional test behavior
  • Test Error — test command issues
  • Syntax Error — general parse errors

item.title: [Solution] Bash Integer Expression Expected
item.description:
Bash Integer Expression Expected Fix

The integer expression expected error occurs when Bash tries to evaluate a non-numeric string as an integer in arithmetic or comparison contexts.

What This Error Means

Bash requires integer values for arithmetic operations and numeric comparisons (-eq, -ne, -lt, etc.). When a variable contains a string, these operations fail.

Common Causes

  • Variable contains non-numeric characters
  • Command output used directly without validation
  • Empty variable used in arithmetic
  • File path or string accidentally used as number

How to Fix

1. Validate before using as integer

value="abc"
if [[ "$value" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
 echo "Valid number: $((value + 1))"
else
 echo "Not a number"
fi

2. Provide default for empty variables

# WRONG: empty variable
count=""
echo $((count + 1))

# RIGHT: default to 0
count=${count:-0}
echo $((count + 1))

3. Use correct comparison operators

# For integers, use -eq -ne -lt -le -gt -ge
if [ "$a" -eq "$b" ]; then echo "Equal"; fi

# For strings, use = != < >
if [ "$a" = "$b" ]; then echo "Equal"; fi

4. Extract numbers from strings

str="File count: 42"
num=$(echo "$str" | grep -o '[0-9]*')
echo $((num + 1))

Related Errors

  • Binary Operator Expected — test expression issues
  • Arithmetic Error — arithmetic syntax issues

item.title: [Solution] Bash Integer Expression Expected Error Fix
item.description:
Bash Integer Expression Expected Error Fix

A bash integer expression expected error occurs when you use arithmetic operators (-eq, -ne, etc.) with non-numeric values.

What This Error Means

Bash arithmetic and comparison operators require integer values. If you pass a string or empty value, bash reports “integer expression expected.” This commonly happens with unset variables or variables containing non-numeric data.

Common Causes

  • Comparing non-numeric strings with -eq
  • Unset or empty variables in test expressions
  • Variables containing spaces or special characters
  • Using [ instead of [[ for comparisons

How to Fix

1. Use [[ ]] for safer comparisons

# WRONG: [ ] doesn't handle empty vars well
value=""
[ "$value" -eq 0 ] # Error: integer expression expected

# CORRECT: Use [[ ]] which handles this
[[ "$value" == 0 ]]

2. Validate before comparing

# CORRECT: Check if numeric first
value="abc"
if [[ "$value" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
 echo "Number: $value"
else
 echo "Not a number: $value"
fi

3. Use default values for unset variables

# WRONG: Unset variable
count=""
if [ "$count" -gt 0 ]; then # Error

# CORRECT: Provide default
count=${count:-0}
if [[ "$count" -gt 0 ]]; then
 echo "Has items"
fi

4. Use case or regex for validation

# CORRECT: Validate input
read -r input
case "$input" in
 ''|*[!0-9]*) echo "Not a valid number" ;;
 *) echo "Number: $input" ;;
esac

Related Errors

  • Arithmetic Error — math operations
  • Conditional Expression — test conditions
  • Bash Test Error — test command issues

item.title: [Solution] Bash Syntax Error in Expression (Arithmetic Error)
item.description:
Bash Syntax Error in Expression (Arithmetic Error) Fix

This error occurs when Bash encounters an invalid arithmetic expression inside $(( )), let, (( )), or expr.

Description

Bash evaluates arithmetic expressions using integer math. When the expression contains syntax errors — such as unmatched parentheses, invalid operators, or non-numeric values — the evaluation fails.

Common Causes

  • Unmatched parentheses — $(( 3 + (2 * 1)) missing a closing paren.
  • Division by zero — $(( 10 / 0 )).
  • Non-numeric values — let result=$var + 1 where $var contains text.
  • Using Bash 3 syntax in newer versions — or vice versa, with incompatible constructs.

How to Fix

Fix 1: Balance all parentheses

# Wrong
echo $(( 3 + (2 * 1) ))

# Right
echo $(( 3 + (2 * 1) ))
# Or more complex
echo $(( (3 + 2) * 1 ))

Fix 2: Guard against division by zero

divisor=0
if [[ $divisor -ne 0 ]]; then
 result=$(( 10 / divisor ))
else
 echo "Cannot divide by zero"
fi

Fix 3: Validate variables are numeric before arithmetic

if [[ "$var" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
 result=$(( var + 1 ))
else
 echo "Not a number: $var"
fi

Fix 4: Use expr for safe arithmetic with error handling

expr 10 + 2 2>&1 || echo "Arithmetic failed"

Examples

$ echo $(( 3 + ))
bash: 3 + : syntax error in expression

$ echo $(( 10 / 0 ))
bash: division by 0

$ let result="abc" + 1
bash: let: result="abc" + 1: syntax error in expression

Related Errors

  • Bad Substitution — incorrect variable expansion in arithmetic contexts.
  • Conditional Expression Expected — errors in test expressions.

item.title: [Solution] Bash Test Command Error
item.description:
Bash Test Command Error Fix

Test command errors occur when [ or test is used with incorrect syntax, wrong operators, or mismatched brackets.

What This Error Means

The test builtin and [ command evaluate conditional expressions. They require specific syntax: exactly one ] to close the expression, correct operators, and proper argument count.

Common Causes

  • Missing closing ] bracket
  • Space between [ and expression or ]
  • Wrong operator for the test type
  • Using == in [ instead of = (bash-specific)

How to Fix

1. Ensure matching brackets with spaces

# WRONG: missing spaces
if [$var = "hello"]; then

# RIGHT: spaces required around [ and ]
if [ "$var" = "hello" ]; then

2. Use = not == in [ ]

# WRONG: == doesn't work in [ ]
if [ $var == "hello" ]; then

# RIGHT: use =
if [ "$var" = "hello" ]; then

# Or use [[ ]] which supports ==
if [[ $var == "hello" ]]; then

3. Use [[ ]] for modern bash

# [[ ]] is safer and more feature-rich
if [[ -f "$file" && -r "$file" ]]; then
 echo "File exists and is readable"
fi

4. Match operator to comparison type

# Numeric comparisons use -eq, -ne, -lt, etc.
if [ "$a" -eq "$b" ]; then echo "Equal"; fi

# String comparisons use =, !=, <, >
if [ "$a" = "$b" ]; then echo "Equal"; fi

Related Errors

  • Binary Operator Expected — operator argument issues
  • Conditional Expression Error — conditional errors

item.title: [Solution] Bash Test: Expression Expected Error Fix
item.description:
Bash Test: Expression Expected Error Fix

A bash test expression error occurs when the test command or [ ] bracket syntax has malformed or empty expressions.

What This Error Means

The test command (or [ ]) evaluates conditional expressions. If the expression is empty, has mismatched brackets, or uses invalid operators, bash reports “expression expected.”

Common Causes

  • Empty expression: [ ] or test
  • Mismatched brackets: [[ "a" -eq "b" ]
  • Using ( inside [ ] instead of $(( ))
  • Wrong operators for string vs numeric comparison

How to Fix

1. Always provide an expression

# WRONG: Empty test
value=""
if [ ]; then # Expression expected

# CORRECT: Proper expression
if [ -n "$value" ]; then
 echo "Has value"
fi

2. Match brackets correctly

# WRONG: Missing bracket
[[ "a" == "b" ]

# CORRECT: Both brackets present
[[ "a" == "b" ]]

3. Use correct operators for types

# WRONG: String operator for numeric
[ "5" == "5" ] # Works but not recommended for numbers
[ "5" -eq "5" ] # Numeric comparison

# CORRECT: Use appropriate operators
[[ "hello" == "hello" ]] # String comparison
[[ 5 -eq 5 ]] # Numeric comparison

4. Use [[ ]] for advanced tests

# CORRECT: [[ ]] supports regex and pattern matching
[[ "$email" =~ ^[a-z]+@[a-z]+\.[a-z]+$ ]] && echo "Valid email"
[[ "$file" == *.log ]] && echo "Log file"

Related Errors

  • Unary Operator Expected — single operand missing
  • Binary Operator Expected — two operands missing
  • Conditional Expression — condition evaluation

item.title: [Solution] PHP Match: Unhandled Value Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Match: Unhandled Value Error Fix

A PHP match error occurs when the match expression encounters a value that is not handled by any arm and has no default branch. Unlike switch, match is strict about exhaustive handling.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.0 introduced the match expression as a strict alternative to switch. It uses strict comparison (===) and requires either all possible values to be covered or a default branch. If a value falls through without a match, PHP throws an UnhandledMatchError.

item.title: [Solution] Python SyntaxError — Starred Assignment Target Must Be In List or Tuple
item.description:
SyntaxError — Starred Assignment Target Must Be In List or Tuple

A SyntaxError with the message “starred assignment target must be in a list or tuple” is raised when you use a starred expression (*variable) as a standalone assignment target instead of inside a list or tuple. Star expressions can only appear in unpacking contexts.

Description

Star expressions (*variable) are used for extended iterable unpacking. They capture “remaining” elements from a sequence. However, they must appear inside a list [...], tuple (...), or as part of a function call. Using *variable as a direct assignment target is not allowed.

item.title: [Solution] Python SyntaxError — Walrus Operator Invalid Syntax
item.description:
SyntaxError — Walrus Operator Invalid Syntax

A SyntaxError with the message “invalid syntax” when using the walrus operator := is raised when you use this operator in a context where it’s not allowed, or when using it in Python versions before 3.8. The walrus operator was introduced in Python 3.8 (PEP 572).

Description

The walrus operator := allows assignment expressions inside other expressions. It assigns a value to a variable as part of a larger expression. However, it has restrictions: it cannot be used at the top level, in function arguments (in some contexts), or in certain other positions.

item.title: [Solution] R Error — Error in Eval Fix
item.description:
Error in Eval — Fix

The error Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos) : ... occurs when R fails to evaluate an expression. This is often a wrapper error that hides the real cause, such as undefined variables or invalid operations.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: Undefined variable in expression
eval(parse(text = "x + 1")) # Error in eval: object 'x' not found

# Cause 2: Invalid expression
eval(parse(text = "1 + + 2")) # Error in eval: unexpected '+'

# Cause 3: Error in data frame operations
df <- data.frame(x = 1:3)
eval(df$x + non_existent) # Error in eval

# Cause 4: Expression referencing missing objects
expr <- quote(y * 2)
eval(expr) # Error in eval: object 'y' not found

How to Fix

Fix 1: Define variables before eval

# Wrong
eval(parse(text = "x + 1"))

# Correct
x <- 10
eval(parse(text = "x + 1")) # Works: returns 11

Fix 2: Use tryCatch for safe evaluation

# Wrong
result <- eval(parse(text = user_input))

# Correct
result <- tryCatch(
 eval(parse(text = user_input)),
 error = function(e) {
 cat("Evaluation error:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
 NULL
 }
)

Fix 3: Check environment before eval

# Wrong
eval(quote(x + y)) # y not defined

# Correct
x <- 5
y <- 3
eval(quote(x + y)) # Works: returns 8

Fix 4: Use local environment for isolation

# Wrong — pollutes global environment
eval(parse(text = "result <- complex_calculation()"))

# Correct — use local environment
env <- new.env()
env$x <- 10
env$y <- 20
result <- eval(quote(x + y), envir = env) # result: 30

Examples

# Example 1: Missing variable in eval
eval(parse(text = "my_var + 1"))
# Error in eval(parse(text = "my_var + 1")) : object 'my_var' not found

# Example 2: Invalid expression
eval(parse(text = "if TRUE print('yes')"))
# Error in eval(parse(text = "if TRUE print('yes')")) : unexpected symbol

# Example 3: eval with data
df <- data.frame(a = 1:5, b = 6:10)
result <- eval(quote(a + b), envir = df)
# Returns: 7, 9, 11, 13, 15

# Example 4: Nested eval
x <- 5
eval(eval(quote(x * 2))) # Works: returns 10

Related Errors

  • object-not-found — undefined variable
  • error-in-parse — syntax errors
  • error-in-source — sourcing files

item.title: [Solution] SQL Not a GROUP BY Expression Fix
item.description:
This error occurs when a column in the SELECT list is neither part of the GROUP BY clause nor wrapped in an aggregate function. It is a variant of the GROUP BY error.

What This Error Means

With ONLY_FULL_GROUP_BY mode, every column in SELECT must either appear in GROUP BY or be used inside an aggregate function like COUNT, SUM, MAX, MIN, or AVG.

Common Causes

  • SELECT includes a raw column without grouping by it
  • Using SELECT * with GROUP BY
  • Mixing aggregated and non-aggregated data incorrectly

How to Fix

Fix 1: Include the column in GROUP BY

-- Wrong
SELECT department, employee_name, COUNT(*)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

-- Correct
SELECT department, employee_name, COUNT(*)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department, employee_name;

Fix 2: Use an aggregate function

SELECT department, MAX(employee_name), COUNT(*)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

Fix 3: Use ANY_VALUE

SELECT department, ANY_VALUE(employee_name), COUNT(*)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

Examples

SELECT category, title, COUNT(*)
FROM products
GROUP BY category;
-- ERROR 1055: Not a GROUP BY expression

Related Errors

  • GROUP BY Error — related GROUP BY issue
  • No GROUP BY — missing GROUP BY clause

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a syntax error indicating TypeScript expected an expression at a position where none was found. This typically happens with malformed function calls, object literals, or template literals.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing argument in function call
doSomething(); // if function expects args and comma is missing
doSomething(, "hello"); // TS1109: expression expected before comma

// Cause 2: Empty template literal issue
const msg = `Hello, ${}`; // TS1109: expression expected inside ${}

// Cause 3: Invalid object syntax
const obj = {
 name: "Alice"
 age: 30 // TS1109 if comma missing
};

// Cause 4: Malformed ternary
const x = true ? ; // TS1109: expression expected after ?

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check for missing expressions

// Remove extra commas
doSomething("hello");

// Fill in template literal
const msg = `Hello, ${name}`;

Fix 2: Add missing commas in objects

const obj = {
 name: "Alice",
 age: 30
};

Fix 3: Complete ternary expressions

const x = true ? "yes" : "no";

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — missing semicolon.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — missing declaration.
  • TS1136: Expected block of statements — missing block.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression Expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected an expression. This usually happens with malformed function calls, missing operands, or incomplete code.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Empty function call argument
const result = Math.random() // Missing closing paren or argument

// Cause 2: Missing right operand
let x = 5 + ; // TS1109: expression expected after '+'

// Cause 3: Incomplete arrow function
const fn = () => // TS1109: expression expected after '=>'

// Cause 4: Missing value in assignment
const obj = {
 key: // TS1109: expression expected after ':'
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Complete the expression

// Wrong
let x = 5 + ;

// Correct
let x = 5 + 10;

Fix 2: Close parentheses and provide arguments

// Wrong
const result = Math.random()

// Correct
const result = Math.random();

Fix 3: Provide a return value for arrow functions

// Wrong
const fn = () =>

// Correct
const fn = () => 42;

// Or with block body
const fn = () => {
 return 42;
};

Fix 4: Complete object property values

// Wrong
const obj = {
 key:
};

// Correct
const obj = {
 key: "value"
};

Examples

// Example 1: Incomplete template literal
const name = `Hello, ${; // TS1109

// Fix:
const name = `Hello, ${user.name}`;

// Example 2: Missing argument in new expression
const el = new HTMLElement(); // If HTMLElement isn't defined
// TS1109 if the expression can't be resolved

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader token error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2349 — This expression is not callable
item.description:
TS2349 — This expression is not callable

TS2349 occurs when you try to call an expression as a function, but its type does not include a call signature. The expression is not a function or callable object.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling a non-function variable
const name: string = "Alice";
name(); // TS2349: This expression is not callable

// Cause 2: Importing a module but calling the wrong thing
import config from "./config";
config(); // TS230 if config is an object, not a function

// Cause 3: Calling a class as a function
class Foo {}
Foo(); // TS2349 — must use 'new'

// Cause 4: Overloaded function not callable as expected
function create(): string;
function create(n: number): number[];
function create(n?: number): string | number[] {
 return n ? [] : "";
}
create; // TS2349 if accessed without calling

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify the variable is a function

const greet = (name: string) => `Hello, ${name}`;
greet("Alice"); // OK

Fix 2: Use ’new’ for class instantiation

const instance = new Foo();

Fix 3: Check import name

import { createConfig } from "./config";
createConfig(); // call the actual function

Related Errors

  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — calling a type as a value.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — argument count mismatch.
